words.shortDefinition: Software architecture where a single instance serves multiple customers.
words.longDefinition: Multi-tenancy is an architecture in which a single instance of a software application serves multiple customers (tenants), while ensuring that each tenant's data is isolated and invisible to other tenants.
words.techContext: Cloud ERP providers like NetSuite or Workday use multi-tenancy to push updates to all customers simultaneously while maintaining data security.
words.exampleSentence: The multi-tenancy model allows us to benefit from the latest ERP features without managing individual server upgrades.

words.shortDefinition: Electronic exchange of business documents between organizations.
words.longDefinition: Electronic Data Interchange (EDI) is the computer-to-computer exchange of business documents, such as purchase orders and invoices, in a standard electronic format between business partners.
words.techContext: ERP systems use EDI to automate the supply chain, allowing a retailer's ERP to talk directly to a manufacturer's ERP without human intervention.
words.exampleSentence: We set up an EDI 850 transaction set to automate the transmission of purchase orders to our primary supplier.

words.shortDefinition: A system that manages and executes modeled business processes.
words.longDefinition: A workflow engine is a software application that manages business processes by routing tasks to the appropriate users or systems based on predefined business rules.
words.techContext: In an ERP, the workflow engine handles the approval chain for high-value purchase orders, ensuring they are signed off by the correct managers.
words.exampleSentence: The ERP's workflow engine automatically escalated the invoice for approval when it remained pending for more than 48 hours.

words.shortDefinition: Adapting software to meet the legal and cultural requirements of a specific region.
words.longDefinition: Localization in ERP involves configuring the system to comply with local tax laws (like VAT or GST), accounting standards, and language requirements of a specific country.
words.techContext: Global ERP rollouts require extensive localization to handle the unique electronic invoicing requirements of countries like Brazil or Italy.
words.exampleSentence: We had to implement a new localization package to support our subsidiary's expansion into the Japanese market.

words.shortDefinition: Financial exchange between two entities within the same parent company.
words.longDefinition: An intercompany transaction occurs when one unit of an enterprise trades goods or services with another unit of the same enterprise, requiring specific accounting to avoid double-counting.
words.techContext: The ERP must track intercompany transactions carefully to ensure that internal sales are eliminated during the consolidation process.
words.exampleSentence: Our US entity sold consulting services to our UK branch as an intercompany transaction.

words.shortDefinition: Removing intercompany transactions during financial consolidation.
words.longDefinition: Elimination is the accounting process where transactions between subsidiaries of the same company are removed so they don't appear in the consolidated financial statements.
words.techContext: The ERP automated the elimination entries, preventing our internal software transfers from being counted as external revenue.
words.exampleSentence: We need to run the elimination rules to clean up our balance sheet for the quarterly report.
words.etymology: From Latin 'eliminare', to turn out of doors.

words.shortDefinition: Profits or losses resulting from currency exchange rate fluctuations.
words.longDefinition: Foreign Exchange (FX) Gains and Losses occur when a transaction is recorded in one currency but settled in another, and the exchange rate changes in the interim.
words.techContext: The ERP automatically calculates unrealized FX gains at the end of the month based on the current market exchange rate.
words.exampleSentence: The company reported a significant FX loss due to the sudden devaluation of the Euro against the Dollar.
words.etymology: FX is an abbreviation for Foreign Exchange.

words.shortDefinition: The primary currency of the economic environment in which an entity operates.
words.longDefinition: The functional currency is the currency of the primary economic environment in which a business entity operates and generates/expends cash.
words.techContext: While our subsidiary is in Mexico, their functional currency is USD because all their contracts and expenses are tied to the US market.
words.exampleSentence: We changed the functional currency of the Singapore office to SGD to better reflect their local operations.

words.shortDefinition: Converting financial statements from functional currency to reporting currency.
words.longDefinition: Translation is the accounting process of converting the financial results of a foreign subsidiary from its functional currency into the reporting currency of the parent company.
words.techContext: ERP systems use different exchange rates (average for income statement, spot for balance sheet) during the translation process.
words.exampleSentence: The translation adjustment is recorded in the Other Comprehensive Income (OCI) section of the balance sheet.

words.shortDefinition: Accounting principle determining when revenue is officially recorded.
words.longDefinition: Revenue Recognition is a cornerstone of accrual accounting that stipulates revenue is recognized when it is earned and realizable, regardless of when cash is received.
words.techContext: ERPs use revenue recognition engines to manage complex multi-year software contracts, spreading the revenue across the life of the subscription.
words.exampleSentence: Under ASC 606, we cannot recognize the full revenue of the contract until the performance obligations are met.

words.shortDefinition: Recording expenses or revenues before the actual cash exchange.
words.longDefinition: An accrual is an accounting entry that records revenue or expenses in the period they occur, even if no cash has changed hands yet.
words.techContext: At the end of the year, the ERP generates accrual entries for utility bills we've incurred but haven't received invoices for yet.
words.exampleSentence: We need to post an accrual for the bonuses earned by employees in December.
words.etymology: From French 'accrue', something grown or added.

words.shortDefinition: The process of finalizing financial records for a specific timeframe.
words.longDefinition: Period close is a series of steps taken by the accounting department to lock down financial transactions for a month or quarter and generate financial statements.
words.techContext: The ERP's period close checklist ensures that all sub-ledgers are reconciled and all journal entries are posted before the books are locked.
words.exampleSentence: We are in the middle of the Q3 period close, so the finance team is unavailable for new projects.

words.shortDefinition: A chronological record of all changes made to data.
words.longDefinition: An audit trail is a step-by-step record by which financial data or transactions can be traced to their source, including who made the change and when.
words.techContext: The auditor examined the ERP's audit trail to see who approved the $50,000 vendor payment last Tuesday.
words.exampleSentence: Because our ERP has a robust audit trail, we can easily see which user modified the inventory safety stock levels.

words.shortDefinition: Internal control designed to prevent fraud and error by distributing tasks.
words.longDefinition: Segregation of Duties (SoD) is a security principle where more than one person is required to complete a business process, such as requiring one person to create a vendor and another to pay them.
words.techContext: We ran an SoD analysis in our ERP to ensure no single user had the permission to both create and approve purchase orders.
words.exampleSentence: Failure to maintain proper segregation of duties was flagged as a major risk during our last internal audit.

words.shortDefinition: System of assigning permissions to users based on their job role.
words.longDefinition: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is an approach to restricting system access to authorized users based on their role within an organization.
words.techContext: The ERP administrator assigned the 'Accounts Payable Clerk' role to the new hire, which automatically granted access to invoices but not the general ledger.
words.exampleSentence: Implementing RBAC allowed us to simplify the user onboarding process by using predefined permission sets.

words.shortDefinition: Setting up an ERP using built-in options without changing code.
words.longDefinition: Configuration involves using the software's native tools and settings to define business rules, workflows, and data structures to meet organizational needs.
words.techContext: We prefer configuration over customization because it makes the ERP much easier to upgrade when the vendor releases new versions.
words.exampleSentence: The consultant spent three weeks on the system configuration, setting up the tax schedules and approval limits.

words.shortDefinition: Modifying an ERP's source code or adding external logic.
words.longDefinition: Customization is the process of modifying an ERP system's core code or building custom scripts to handle unique business requirements that cannot be met through standard configuration.
words.techContext: We had to write a script for customization to handle our company's unique logic for calculating loyalty points on wholesale orders.
words.exampleSentence: Too much customization in the legacy system made it nearly impossible to migrate our data to the new cloud platform.
words.etymology: From 'custom' (customary or habitual) and the suffix '-ization'.

words.shortDefinition: A third-party application that integrates with an ERP to add specific functionality.
words.longDefinition: A bolt-on is a specialized software application that is added to an existing ERP system to provide specific niche functionality that the core ERP lacks.
words.techContext: We added a specialized sales tax bolt-on to handle the thousands of different local tax jurisdictions across the US.
words.exampleSentence: The shipping bolt-on allows our warehouse team to print FedEx labels directly from the ERP sales order screen.
words.etymology: Metaphor from mechanics, referring to a part that can be bolted onto a machine.

words.shortDefinition: Using the best specialized software for each function instead of one single ERP.
words.longDefinition: Best-of-Breed is a strategy where an organization selects the best software for each specific business function (e.g., Salesforce for CRM, Workday for HR) and integrates them, rather than using a single integrated ERP suite.
words.techContext: While a single ERP is easier to manage, our company chose a best-of-breed approach to get the most advanced features in our marketing department.
words.exampleSentence: The challenge of the best-of-breed strategy is maintaining the complex integrations between all the different systems.
words.etymology: From animal breeding, referring to the finest specimen in a category.

words.shortDefinition: The process of moving data from an old system to a new ERP.
words.longDefinition: Data Migration is the process of selecting, preparing, extracting, and transforming data and permanently transferring it from one computer storage system to another.
words.techContext: The data migration team spent months cleaning up historical sales records before importing them into the new production environment.
words.exampleSentence: A successful data migration is the most critical factor in ensuring the new ERP is usable on day one.
words.etymology: From 'data' and 'migration' (Latin 'migratio', a change of abode).

words.shortDefinition: Process of moving data from source systems to a target database.
words.longDefinition: Extract, Transform, and Load (ETL) is a three-phase process where data is extracted from various sources, transformed into a clean format, and loaded into a destination system like a data warehouse.
words.techContext: Our ERP uses an nightly ETL job to push sales data into our PowerBI reporting warehouse.
words.exampleSentence: The ETL process failed last night because of a change in the vendor's CSV file format.

words.shortDefinition: Expert who specializes in configuring specific ERP modules.
words.longDefinition: A functional consultant bridges the gap between business requirements and technical implementation, specializing in the logic and configuration of specific modules like Finance or Supply Chain.
words.techContext: The functional consultant mapped our existing manufacturing processes to the ERP's standard work order flow.
words.exampleSentence: We hired a functional consultant to help us optimize our warehouse picking strategies within the system.

words.shortDefinition: Expert who specializes in coding, integration, and database management for ERP.
words.longDefinition: A technical consultant focuses on the underlying technology of the ERP, including custom development, system integrations, and performance tuning.
words.techContext: The technical consultant is responsible for building the API integration between our ERP and the shipping carrier.
words.exampleSentence: Our technical consultant wrote a custom SQL script to fix the corrupted inventory records.
words.etymology: From 'technical' (Greek 'tekhnikos', relating to art or craft) and 'consultant'.

words.shortDefinition: A non-production environment used for testing and training.
words.longDefinition: A sandbox is a separate instance of the ERP system that contains a copy of production data, allowing users to test new configurations or customizations without affecting live operations.
words.techContext: We always test new payroll configurations in the sandbox before moving them to the production environment.
words.exampleSentence: The trainees were given access to the sandbox environment to practice entering sales orders.
words.etymology: From the box of sand for children to play in, representing a safe, contained area.

words.shortDefinition: The point at which a new ERP system becomes the system of record.
words.longDefinition: Go-Live is the moment an organization switches from its old system or manual processes to the new ERP system for its daily business operations.
words.techContext: During the Go-Live weekend, the IT team worked around the clock to perform the final data cutover and verify system stability.
words.exampleSentence: Our Go-Live date is set for January 1st to align with the start of the new fiscal year.
words.etymology: Descriptive phrase for when a system 'goes live'.
